questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with shortness of breath on exertion for 6 months. On examination, his pulse is bounding and collapsing, blood pressure 160/50 mmHg. There is a high-pitched early diastolic murmur heard at the left sternal border. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of collapsing pulse, wide pulse pressure, and early diastolic murmur is classic for chronic severe aortic regurgitation. Patent ductus arteriosus gives a continuous murmur, and thyrotoxicosis does not produce a diastolic murmur.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old intravenous drug user presents with acute onset dyspnoea and chest pain. He is tachycardic and hypotensive. There is a soft, short early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge. The jugular venous pressure is elevated and there are bibasal crackles. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Acute severe aortic regurgitation presents with a soft murmur due to rapid pressure equalisation, tachycardia, and acute heart failure. Acute mitral regurgitation typically has a pan systolic murmur, not early diastolic.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man is noted to have a murmur on routine medical examination. He is asymptomatic. Blood pressure 120/80 mmHg. There is a short, early diastolic murmur at the left sternal border that becomes louder on expiration. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Mild aortic regurgitation presents with a soft early diastolic murmur. Pulmonary regurgitation (Graham Steell) is associated with pulmonary hypertension and a loud P2. Benign flow murmurs are systolic. Mitral stenosis has a diastolic rumble, and VSD is pan systolic.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known chronic aortic regurgitation for 5 years presents with progressive dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and ankle swelling. On examination, he has bilateral pitting oedema, elevated JVP, and a displaced apex beat. There is a high-pitched early diastolic murmur. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of a diastolic murmur confirms aortic regurgitation as the underlying valve lesion causing heart failure. Aortic stenosis would have a systolic murmur, and dilated cardiomyopathy typically has no murmur.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with a history of low back pain and morning stiffness for 10 years presents with a new murmur. He is otherwise well. Examination reveals a diastolic murmur at the left sternal border and a blood pressure of 150/50 mmHg. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of back pain (suggesting ankylosing spondylitis) and a diastolic murmur with wide pulse pressure points to aortic regurgitation due to aortitis. Aortic stenosis is systolic, mitral stenosis has a diastolic rumble, and infective endocarditis would have fever and signs of infection.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with dyspnoea on exertion. Examination reveals a collapsing pulse and a high-pitched early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm structural aortic regurgitation?
questions.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) with Doppler is the first-line investigation to visualise the aortic valve and quantify regurgitation. Chest X-ray and ECG may show secondary changes but do not confirm the diagnosis. TOE and MRI are reserved for when TTE is inadequate.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with known mild aortic regurgitation develops new fatigue and mild dyspnoea. TTE shows poor image quality due to obesity. What is the most appropriate next test to assess the severity of his aortic regurgitation?
questions.explanation: When TTE is non-diagnostic, transoesophageal echocardiography (TOE) provides superior views of the aortic valve and can accurately quantify regurgitation. Cardiac MRI is an alternative but is not first-line after TTE. Repeat TTE is unlikely to improve image quality without intervention.

questions.stem: Which of the following echocardiographic criteria is most consistent with severe chronic aortic regurgitation?
options: Holodiastolic flow reversal in the descending aorta
questions.explanation: Holodiastolic flow reversal in the descending aorta is a specific sign of severe aortic regurgitation. The other options represent moderate or mild severity: vena contracta >0.6 cm, pressure half-time <200 ms, regurgitant volume >60 ml, and jet width/LVOT ratio >60% indicate severe disease.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old intravenous drug user presents with fever, splinter haemorrhages, and a new early diastolic murmur. Bedside TTE shows a possible mobile mass on the aortic valve but is limited. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the presence of aortic regurgitation and its cause?
questions.explanation: In suspected infective endocarditis with poor TTE window, transoesophageal echocardiography (TOE) is superior for detecting vegetations and leaflet damage, and can accurately diagnose aortic regurgitation. CT aortogram and catheterisation are not indicated for valve assessment. Blood cultures are important but do not diagnose AR.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman (32 weeks gestation) with known bicuspid aortic valve reports increasing breathlessness. Examination suggests moderate aortic regurgitation. Which is the safest and most appropriate investigation to evaluate the severity of her aortic regurgitation?
options: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging without gadolinium
questions.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) is safe in pregnancy and is the first-line imaging for assessing aortic regurgitation. TOE is more invasive and carries a small risk, while MRI without gadolinium is safe but not first-line. Chest X-ray and CT involve radiation exposure and are not appropriate for initial evaluation.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with known chronic severe aortic regurgitation presents with progressive exertional dyspnoea (NYHA III). Echocardiography shows severe AR with LVEF 55% and left ventricular end-systolic diameter 52 mm. He has no significant coronary disease. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
questions.explanation: In severe chronic AR with symptoms (NYHA III) and LV dilatation, surgical AVR is indicated. Medical therapy (ramipril, amlodipine) is not definitive, and beta-blockers are relatively contraindicated. Watchful waiting is inappropriate given symptoms.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a bicuspid aortic valve presents with acute dyspnoea, fever, and a new diastolic murmur. He is hypotensive (85/50 mmHg) with pulmonary oedema. Blood cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus. Echocardiography shows severe acute aortic regurgitation. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
questions.explanation: Acute severe AR with cardiogenic shock requires urgent surgical intervention. Vasodilators and inotropes may be used as a bridge, but definitive management is emergency valve replacement. Antibiotics alone are insufficient.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a history of bicuspid aortic valve presents with fever, rigors, and a new AR murmur. Transoesophageal echocardiography reveals a 12 mm vegetation on the aortic valve, severe AR, and a 5 mm aortic root abscess. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Aortic valve replacement with debridement of abscess
questions.explanation: In IE complicated by aortic root abscess, urgent surgery is indicated even if stable, to prevent progression. Antibiotics alone are insufficient. TAVI is not for AR, and corticosteroids are contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with chronic severe aortic regurgitation presents with worsening dyspnoea. Echocardiography shows severe AR, LVEF 30%, and left ventricular end-systolic diameter 55 mm. He has no coronary artery disease. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Optimised medical therapy with ramipril, bisoprolol, and spironolactone
questions.explanation: Even with low LVEF, surgical AVR is recommended for severe AR as it improves symptoms and survival. Medical therapy alone is not definitive, and TAVI is for aortic stenosis. Heart transplantation is reserved for end-stage disease despite AVR.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with moderate-to-severe chronic aortic regurgitation and well-controlled hypertension (BP 130/80 mmHg) is being reviewed. Which of the following drugs is relatively contraindicated in his condition?
questions.explanation: Beta-blockers prolong diastole, increasing the time for regurgitant flow, and can worsen AR. They are relatively contraindicated. ACE inhibitors, calcium-channel blockers, and other antihypertensives are safe and may reduce afterload.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with hypertension is found to have moderate aortic regurgitation on echocardiography performed for left ventricular hypertrophy assessment. He is asymptomatic with normal left ventricular function. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend initiating vasodilator therapy (ACE inhibitor or ARB) in asymptomatic aortic regurgitation with hypertension to reduce afterload and slow progression. Repeat echo is appropriate but not immediate; surgery is not indicated without symptoms or LV dysfunction.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known bicuspid aortic valve and moderate aortic regurgitation presents with acute breathlessness. He has a new diastolic murmur and signs of acute pulmonary oedema. Temperature 38.5°C. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer for urgent aortic valve replacement
questions.explanation: Acute pulmonary oedema is life-threatening and must be managed first with diuretics. While infective endocarditis is likely (fever, new murmur), antibiotics and definitive imaging follow stabilization. Nitroprusside is an option but diuretic is first-line for fluid overload.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with chronic severe aortic regurgitation on annual surveillance echocardiography has developed symptoms of dyspnoea on minimal exertion. Her left ventricular ejection fraction has dropped to 45% with left ventricular end-systolic diameter of 52 mm. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Symptomatic severe aortic regurgitation or asymptomatic with LVEF <50% or LVESD >50mm is an indication for aortic valve replacement per UK guidelines. Medical therapy is not sufficient; surgery is definitive.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with Marfan syndrome is found to have mild aortic regurgitation on a screening echocardiogram. His aortic root diameter is 40 mm. He is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Beta-blockers reduce the rate of aortic root dilation in Marfan syndrome and are first-line prophylaxis. Surgery is considered when aortic root diameter >50mm (or >45mm with risk factors). Repeat echo is important but the immediate recommendation is to start beta-blocker therapy.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman with known moderate aortic regurgitation presents with cough, fever, and acute dyspnoea. She has bibasal crackles, raised jugular venous pressure, and blood pressure 100/60 mmHg. Chest X-ray shows consolidation and pulmonary oedema. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Pulmonary oedema is the immediate threat; diuresis with furosemide is the priority. Antibiotics for pneumonia are important but not as urgent as treating heart failure. Fluids are contraindicated. Imaging and echo can be done after stabilization.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old intravenous drug user presents with fever, rigors, and acute dyspnoea. On examination, there is a short early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge and a palpable left ventricular heave. Auscultation of the heart sounds reveals a normal S2 but a very quiet S1. Which single clinical finding in this patient suggests acute severe aortic regurgitation rather than chronic compensated disease?
questions.explanation: In acute severe AR, the left ventricular diastolic pressure rises rapidly, causing premature closure of the mitral valve, which softens S1. In chronic compensated AR, classic signs are wide pulse pressure and bounding pulses; the Austin Flint murmur is a mid-diastolic murmur heard in chronic severe AR. An S3 gallop may indicate LV failure but does not distinguish acute from chronic.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with chronic severe aortic regurgitation due to bicuspid valve (known for 5 years, previously asymptomatic) presents with worsening exertional dyspnoea over the last month. His blood pressure is 150/50 mmHg, pulse 90 bpm. On auscultation, you hear a high-pitched early diastolic murmur and a new low-frequency sound in early diastole after the murmur. Which single auscultatory finding most strongly indicates that he has developed left ventricular failure?
questions.explanation: An S3 gallop indicates left ventricular volume overload and failure, common in decompensated severe AR. An S4 gallop reflects a stiff LV (hypertrophy) and is more typical of aortic stenosis. Split S2 is normal in many, loud S1 is unexpected (chronic AR often has soft S1), and an opening snap suggests mitral stenosis.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old male with a history of hypertension and a strong family history of aortic aneurysm presents with sudden onset of severe tearing chest pain radiating to his back. On examination, his right arm BP is 190/100 mmHg and left arm BP is 150/80 mmHg. Cardiac auscultation reveals a new early diastolic murmur. Which single clinical finding most strongly points to aortic dissection rather than other causes of acute aortic regurgitation?
questions.explanation: A significant blood pressure discrepancy between arms (>20 mmHg) is a classic sign of aortic dissection causing asymmetry of flow. While radiating chest pain and a new diastolic murmur are suggestive, they are less specific. Hypertension is common but not diagnostic, and collapsing pulse is typical of chronic severe AR, not dissection.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman (30 weeks gestation) with known mild-to-moderate aortic regurgitation presents with acute onset of breathlessness. She has a history of previous valve-sparing aortic root replacement. On examination, she is tachypnoeic, her BP is 120/40 mmHg, and she has a low-pitched diastolic murmur. Which single clinical sign would indicate that the aortic regurgitation is now severe and requires urgent obstetric and cardiology review?
options: Bilateral basal crackles on lung auscultation
questions.explanation: Bilateral basal crackles indicate pulmonary oedema from acute left ventricular failure, a red flag in pregnancy with AR. In pregnancy, hyperdynamic circulation can mimic signs of severe AR (e.g., low diastolic BP, collapsing pulse), but crackles directly reflect decompensation. A palpable LV apex and RV heave are non-specific.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with ankylosing spondylitis presents for routine review. He has known mild aortic regurgitation. He is asymptomatic. Lately, he has noticed occasional light-headedness but no syncope. ECG shows a prolonged PR interval and a new left bundle branch block. Which single finding best indicates possible progression of his aortitis to involve the conduction system, requiring urgent echocardiography?
options: Left bundle branch block on ECG
questions.explanation: New left bundle branch block (LBBB) suggests conduction system involvement from aortitis and is an ominous sign that warrants urgent echo to assess aortic root dimensions and valve function. Prolonged PR interval (first-degree AV block) is common and less specific. QT prolongation, ST elevation, and Q waves are not typically associated with aortitis.
